question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a check that the number of distinct nodes in the VERIFIED group is greater than one, so that a single node submitting multiple StreamFileSignature objects with the same fileHash but different node references (all pointing to the same physical node) inflates the stake count, satisfying the 1/3 threshold with a single node's actual stake, leading to false consensus and direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: getStakeRequiredForConsensus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the RoundingMode.CEILING in getStakeRequiredForConsensus() (line 99) combined with a carefully chosen totalStake value to make the required threshold exactly 1 tinybar higher than the attacker's coalition stake, causing a legitimate consensus to be rejected, while simultaneously crafting a second scenario where CEILING rounds down to the attacker's stake, accepting a fraudulent consensus, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a collection where the first signature's node has totalStake = 1 (the minimum non-zero value), causing getStakeRequiredForConsensus() to compute a threshold of 1 (since 1 * 1/3 rounds up to 1), and then any single VERIFIED signature with stake >= 1 satisfies consensus, allowing a single attacker-controlled node to unilaterally accept any stream file, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the debugStake variable (line 59) only tracks the maximum stake group for error reporting, not for consensus logic, so that an attacker can craft a collection where the highest-stake group has a fraudulent fileHash and the legitimate group has lower stake, causing the error message to mislead operators about which group was closest to consensus, delaying incident response and enabling sustained direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the mutable SignatureStatus enum on StreamFileSignature objects by pre-setting signatures to CONSENSUS_REACHED status before passing them to validate(), causing the status check at line 49 (which only checks for VERIFIED) to skip these signatures from the signatureHashMap, while downstream code trusts the pre-set CONSENSUS_REACHED status, bypassing the actual consensus check and leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Collection interface allowing null elements, so that a null StreamFileSignature in the collection causes a NullPointerException at line 49 (signature.getStatus()) that is not caught within validate(), propagating up and aborting the validation of a legitimate stream file, causing a denial-of-service that prevents the mirror node from processing valid transactions?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not verify that the fileHash in each StreamFileSignature actually matches the content of the downloaded stream file, so that a VERIFIED signature (cryptographically valid against the node's public key) for a different file's hash is included in the collection, and if enough such signatures share the same fraudulent hash, consensus is declared for a file the mirror node never actually downloaded, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a minimum collection size check in validate(), so that passing a collection with exactly one signature whose node has stake >= totalStake * consensusRatio (e.g., a node with 100% of stake) causes immediate consensus with a single node, and by injecting a crafted signature for that dominant node with a fraudulent fileHash, the attacker causes the mirror node to accept falsified transaction records, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the consensusRatio is read from CommonDownloaderProperties at runtime (line 98) without caching, so that if the property is modified between the zero-check at line 54 and the threshold computation at line 98, a TOCTOU race condition causes the threshold to be computed with a different ratio than was checked, potentially allowing consensus bypass, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a collection where all signatures have the same fileHashAsHex (e.g., empty string from null fileHash) but different nodes, causing all stake to be pooled under a single hash group, and if the total stake of all nodes exceeds the threshold, consensus is declared for a null/empty hash rather than a real file hash, causing the mirror node to accept a stream file with no verified content hash, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() iterates over signatureHashMap.keySet() (line 62) and the first group to satisfy canReachConsensus() causes consensusCount to become positive, but the loop continues and may mark additional hash groups as CONSENSUS_REACHED, so that multiple conflicting file hashes are simultaneously marked as consensus-reached, causing the mirror node to ingest contradictory transaction records and leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the validate() method does not check whether the same node appears multiple times in the collection (once per hash group), so that a single compromised node submits signatures for multiple different file hashes, and its stake is counted independently in each group, potentially allowing a single node with >1/3 stake to simultaneously satisfy consensus for multiple conflicting hashes, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that stakeRequiredForConsensus is initialized to null (line 39) and only set when the first signature is processed, so that if the collection's iterator returns a signature with a null node (causing NPE at line 45), the exception propagates without any cleanup, leaving partially-mutated signature statuses in an inconsistent state that downstream code may misinterpret as validated, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the consensus threshold is based on 1/3 of total stake (the minimum Byzantine fault tolerance threshold), so that by compromising or simulating exactly 1/3 of total stake across multiple nodes, an attacker can consistently pass the consensus check for any fraudulent stream file, leading to systematic direct loss of funds across all transactions in the accepted stream?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not verify the cryptographic integrity of the fileHash itself (only that enough nodes signed it), so that if an attacker can cause nodes to sign a hash of a tampered stream file (e.g., through a supply chain attack on the stream file before it reaches the mirror node), the consensus check passes for fraudulent content, leading to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the SignatureVerificationException thrown at line 84 includes the debugStake and totalStake values in its message, leaking internal stake distribution information that an attacker can use to precisely calibrate how much stake they need to compromise to reach the consensus threshold, enabling targeted attacks that lead to direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() accepts a Collection<StreamFileSignature> without validating that all signatures correspond to the same stream file, so that by mixing signatures from different stream files (different filenames) into a single collection, the stake from legitimate signatures for file A is pooled with fraudulent signatures for file B under the same hash group, causing false consensus for file B and direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the consensusCount variable (line 60) is incremented by validatedSignatures.size() (line 71) rather than by 1, so that a single hash group with many duplicate signatures inflates consensusCount far above zero, and the only check is consensusCount > 0 (line 80), meaning the inflation has no effect on the pass/fail decision but could mask the true number of distinct nodes that reached consensus, misleading audit logs and enabling undetected fraud?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the HashMultimap allows the same (fileHashAsHex, StreamFileSignature) pair to be inserted multiple times if the signature object is not equal by reference, causing the stake of a single node to be counted multiple times in the inner loop (line 67), artificially satisfying the consensus threshold with fewer distinct nodes than required, leading to false consensus and direct loss of funds?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/downloader/ConsensusValidator.java -> Scope: Critical: Direct loss of funds'] [Function: validate()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that validate() does not enforce a maximum collection size, so that by supplying an extremely large collection (millions of signatures), the method consumes excessive memory and CPU, causing an OutOfMemoryError or extreme latency that constitutes a denial-of-service, preventing the mirror node from processing legitimate stream files and indirectly enabling direct loss of funds by halting fraud detection?
